content.text: Every React function component goes through a predictable sequence of steps: it is mounted (rendered and committed to the DOM for the first time), may be updated many times (re-rendered when state or props change), and eventually unmounted (removed from the DOM when it is no longer needed). Understanding these phases and the exact order in which React runs effects is the foundation for writing correct and efficient components.

attrs.alt: React component lifecycle: mount, update, unmount phases diagram

content.text: A component mounts the first time it appears in the rendered tree. The mount sequence is:
React calls the component function and receives the JSX tree.
React creates DOM nodes and inserts them into the document.
React runs synchronously before the browser has a chance to paint. Used for DOM measurements that must happen before the user sees anything.
The browser draws the updated pixels to the screen.
React runs asynchronously after the paint. Used for subscriptions, data fetching, and other side effects that do not need to block the paint.

Any change to component state or incoming props triggers a re-render. React re-executes the component function, diffs the new virtual DOM against the previous one, and commits only the changed DOM nodes. Effects whose dependency arrays include the changed value are re-run: their cleanup function runs first, then the setup function runs again.
, then runs the cleanup of the previous
to prevent stale data from being set), and then runs the setup again with the new

When a component is removed from the React tree — because a parent no longer renders it, or a conditional renders false — React runs all effect cleanup functions and then removes the DOM nodes. State is destroyed: the next time the same component type mounts, it starts with initial state values again.
The same cleanup function handles both the update case (when
changes) and the unmount case (when the component is removed). React does not differentiate between the two — the return function is always called before the next setup or on unmount.
Both hooks have the same API, but they run at different points in the commit phase:
Sync, after DOM mutation, before paint
No — runs before frame is shown
only when you need to read DOM measurements (e.g., element width/height) and apply layout corrections before the user sees a flash of incorrect UI.
The dependency array controls when an effect re-runs:
On mount, and whenever a or b changes
When a conditional expression changes from rendering a component to not rendering it, the component unmounts and its state is lost. When it becomes rendered again, it mounts fresh with initial state.

prop forces React to unmount the old instance and mount a completely new one, even if the component type is the same. This is the intentional way to reset a component's state without lifting state up.
Forgetting to clear intervals, cancel fetch requests, or unsubscribe from event emitters causes memory leaks and stale update errors after unmount.
Empty array when deps are missing:
when the effect uses state or props that can change causes stale closures — the effect captures the initial values and never updates.
Omitting the array causes the effect to re-run after every render, often creating infinite loops when the effect itself sets state.
triggers a synchronous re-render before the paint, which can degrade performance.
When multiple effects unmount, React runs their cleanups in the order the hooks were called. Do not rely on a specific cross-effect cleanup order.

React function component lifecycle has three phases: mount (first render, DOM insertion, effects run), update (re-render triggered by state or props, DOM patched, effects re-run for changed deps), and unmount (effect cleanups run, DOM removed, state destroyed). useLayoutEffect runs synchronously before paint; useEffect runs asynchronously after paint. The cleanup function from each effect handles both the update case and the unmount case.
In StrictMode (the default in React 18+ development), React intentionally mounts, unmounts, and re-mounts every component once immediately after its first mount. This means your

setup and cleanup functions each run twice on mount in development. This behaviour exists to expose effects that are not idempotent — if your effect breaks on the second run, it will also break in production when React's concurrent features cause remounting.

Every effect must be safe to run more than once with the same cleanup in between. If running the setup twice causes bugs (duplicate subscriptions, duplicate analytics events), the effect is not correctly cleaned up.

Cleanup exists because side effects often acquire resources that must be released: a timer must be cleared, a subscription must be cancelled, an event listener must be removed, a WebSocket must be closed. Without cleanup, these resources persist even after the component is gone, causing memory leaks and ghost updates.

The cleanup function is the return value of the setup function. React captures this return value and calls it before the next setup or on unmount. If your setup function does not return anything, React treats it as if it returned

React guarantees that the cleanup function runs before the next setup function and before unmount. It does NOT guarantee that cleanup runs before the next render — the cleanup of the previous effect runs asynchronously, scheduled after paint.
Concurrent React and the Render Phase

React 18 introduced concurrent rendering, which can pause, abandon, and restart renders. The render phase is now truly non-blocking: React may start rendering a component, then discard the result and start again if a higher-priority update arrives. The commit phase (and effects) still run exactly once per update.

With concurrent rendering, React can interrupt a render mid-way and restart it. Mutations during render that were meant to happen once can happen zero times, once, or multiple times. Keep the render function pure.
Lifecycle Comparison: Class vs Function Components
If you have used class components, the mapping to hooks is approximate — the mental models differ in important ways:
— initial state is set in the useState call, not a constructor.
— runs once after first commit. Not exact: useEffect runs asynchronously after paint; componentDidMount ran synchronously (similar to useLayoutEffect).
— runs when deps change. Unlike componentDidUpdate, you cannot easily access prevProps/prevState without a useRef workaround.
— the cleanup function runs when the component unmounts.
— wrapping a function component in React.memo provides prop-based bailout from re-rendering.
getDerivedStateFromProps → useMemo or computation during render
— derive values from props inside the component function body.
During development, adding lifecycle-annotated logs helps you understand the order of operations and diagnose unexpected re-renders or missed cleanups.
Note how the cleanup always runs before the next setup. Also note that in StrictMode development, each setup/cleanup pair runs twice on the initial mount.
Async operations started during an effect can complete after the component unmounts. Without cancellation, they may try to call
on an unmounted component — React 18 silently ignores this, but it indicates a resource leak.
AbortController is cleaner than a boolean flag because it also cancels the in-flight network request, freeing bandwidth in addition to suppressing the state update.

Custom hooks encapsulate lifecycle logic so you can share it across components. The lifecycle of the custom hook is tied to the component that calls it — when the host component mounts, the hook's effects mount; when the host unmounts, the hook's cleanups run.

Custom hooks are the primary mechanism for reusing lifecycle logic in React. Each component instance that calls the hook gets its own independent effect instances — the hook is not shared, it is called once per component.
lets you test mount, update, and unmount behaviours by rendering, acting, and asserting:
triggers the component's unmount phase, running all effect cleanups. Asserting that cleanup was called proves the lifecycle logic is correct.

Practice: trace through a component that fetches data on mount, refetches when a prop changes, and cancels in-flight requests on unmount. Write out the exact sequence of render, commit, cleanup, and setup steps for each scenario.
